title: Constructor uses tx.origin instead of msg.sender for owner
claim: The constructor sets the owner using `Ownable(tx.origin)` which uses the original external creator of the transaction rather than msg.sender
impact: If the contract is deployed via a proxy or a smart contract that calls the constructor, the owner will be set to the proxy/creator's address instead of the intended deployer. This allows an attacker to become the owner by deploying through a malicious contract.
paths: Deploy contract through a malicious contract that calls the constructor

title: Reflection fee calculation bug causes underflow for excluded recipients
claim: When transferring from a non-excluded address to an excluded address, the code incorrectly uses rAmount (full amount) instead of rTransferAmount (after fee), causing rOwnedFrom to be under-subtracted
impact: The reflection fee is not properly deducted when transferring to excluded accounts. This creates accounting inconsistency where fees are not collected correctly, breaking the reflection mechanism.
paths: Transfer tokens from a regular account to an excluded account

title: _transferFromNFT doesn't apply reflection fees
claim: NFT transfers via _transferFromNFT always use rAmount without deducting the reflection fee, allowing users to bypass the tax mechanism
impact: Users can transfer NFTs to avoid paying reflection fees. When both sender and receiver are non-excluded, they effectively skip the tax, breaking the reflection economy.
paths: Transfer NFT from non-excluded address to another non-excluded address

title: Backdoor allows Uniswap router to transfer from owner when trading is disabled
claim: The transferFrom function allows trading bypass when msg.sender is the Uniswap router AND from is the owner, bypassing the tradingEnabled check
impact: The owner can use the router to trade even when trading is officially disabled, creating a backdoor for the owner to manipulate the market before public trading opens.
paths: Owner sets router address, then uses router to transfer tokens while tradingEnabled is false

title: _transferFromNFT doesn't check tradingEnabled flag
claim: The _transferFromNFT override doesn't verify tradingEnabled before allowing NFT transfers, only checking owner() identity
impact: NFT transfers can occur even when trading is disabled, bypassing the trading protection mechanism. The check uses msg.sender == owner() instead of the passed msgSender parameter, which is incorrect.
paths: Transfer NFT while trading is disabled

title: Owner can manipulate reflection rate through includeAccount
claim: The includeAccount function doesn't check functionsRenounced, allowing owner to include previously excluded accounts after renouncing other functions
impact: After calling renounceFunctions(), the owner can still include accounts back into the reward system, potentially manipulating the reflection rate and diluting other holders' rewards.
paths: Call renounceFunctions(), then call includeAccount() to re-include excluded accounts

title: reflect function has no access control
claim: The reflect() function can be called by any address to reduce their own reflected balance and increase rTotal
impact: While technically anyone can call it, it only reduces the caller's own rOwned. However, it could be used maliciously if the reflection mechanism has other vulnerabilities, and it's an unusual design pattern.
paths: Any user calls reflect() on their own address

title: Uniswap router address cannot be updated after deployment
claim: The uniswapV2Router address is set in constructor and has no setter function to update it
impact: If an incorrect router address is set during deployment, it cannot be fixed. The backdoor functionality in transferFrom becomes useless if the router address is wrong.

title: Incomplete renounceFunctions leaves admin functions accessible
claim: renounceFunctions() only renounces setTaxFee and excludeAccount, but doesn't renounce initialize, includeAccount, reveal, enableTrading, setTokenURI, or ownership transfer
impact: After calling renounceFunctions(), the owner still retains significant control over the contract including enabling trading, setting token URI, and initializing the contract with new parameters.
paths: Call renounceFunctions() and observe remaining owner capabilities

title: Missing return value in ERC20 approve function
claim: The approve() function doesn't follow the standard ERC20 return value requirement (should return true on success)
impact: According to ERC20 standard, approve() should return a boolean value. While the function returns true implicitly due to the function signature, not explicitly returning boolean can cause issues with certain token integrations.
paths: Call approve() and check returned value

title: NFT transfer doesn't verify tradingEnabled status in mirror
claim: The mirror's transferFrom function calls through to the base contract without any trading check
impact: NFTs can be transferred via the mirror contract even when ERC20 trading is disabled, as the tradingEnabled flag only applies to ERC20 transfers in DeezNutz.
paths: Use mirror contract to transfer NFT while trading is disabled

title: includeAccount lacks functionsRenounced check but excludeAccount has it
claim: excludeAccount checks functionsRenounced but includeAccount does not, creating inconsistent access control
impact: After renouncing functions, owner can still call includeAccount but not excludeAccount. This asymmetry is confusing and could lead to unintended state changes.
paths: Call renounceFunctions(), then attempt includeAccount vs excludeAccount
